title: The shopkeeper who learned my name on day three
summary: Describe an unremarkable corner shop where the owner remembers your name and small preferences, and why that ordinary recognition matters more than it sounds.
topic: A shopkeeper who greets you by name
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a particular shopkeeper, café owner, or vendor who knows you by name, and why this small recognition matters in a big city. Use the present perfect for the relationship over time, a second conditional, a relative clause, and 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: On the third visit to the small grocery shop on the corner of my street in Edinburgh, the owner called me by my first name before I had told him what it was, and I still cannot…
If you have only ever lived in cities where you are anonymous at every cash register, the experience of being recognised by a shopkeeper might sound trivial, but…
Although there is nothing dramatic about a small daily greeting, what I have come to understand is that being known by one person in a city of half a million is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cửa hàng nhỏ ở góc phố
một cái gật đầu thân thiện
một việc mua sắm thường lệ

hintVocab.phrase: to feel part of a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: cảm thấy là một phần của nơi nào đó

sampleText:
On the third visit to the small grocery shop on the corner of my street in Edinburgh, the owner called me by my first name before I had told him what it was. He must have seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before. The greeting was brief — a nod, my name, the price of two items — and would have looked unremarkable to anyone passing the door. I have lived in three cities in the past decade, and I can count on one hand the number of times a shopkeeper has done this in any of them. It is not, on the surface, a generous gesture. But I have never forgotten it, and I now go to that shop in preference to the larger supermarket two streets away even when the supermarket would be quicker and slightly cheaper.

If you have only ever lived in cities where you are anonymous at every cash register, the experience of being recognised by a shopkeeper might sound trivial, like a small piece of nostalgia for village life. It is not trivial. Modern cities are organised around being unknown — by the checkout assistant, by the bus driver, by the pharmacist whose name tag you do not read. Being unknown is convenient, and most of the time it is what we are paying for. However, the cost of that convenience is small and continuous: a hundred tiny daily transactions that confirm, without anyone meaning to, that nobody around you needs to know who you are. The corner shop interrupts that pattern. It does not interrupt it dramatically. It just refuses, in one small place on one street, to participate in the rule.

What I have come to recognise, two years in, is that being known by a shopkeeper is not really about the shopkeeper. It is about the small piece of social infrastructure that a person like him quietly maintains for the people in two surrounding streets. He knows who has moved in and who has moved out. He knows whose mother is in hospital and whose teenager is going through a difficult phase. None of this information is gossiped or weaponised; it simply sits with him, like the stock of tinned tomatoes, available when someone needs to be asked the right question. Although I am sure there are practical reasons to value the larger supermarket — price, range, opening hours — what the supermarket cannot give me is a single person who would notice if I disappeared for three weeks. The corner shop can. In a city, that is rarer than it sounds, and worth defending in the small way I can, which is by continuing to walk past the supermarket and buying my milk from him.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to mark something completed before another past moment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The owner called me by my first name before I had told him what it was' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành sau 'before' để nhấn mạnh rằng hành động trong mệnh đề 'before' chưa xảy ra tại thời điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The owner called me by my first name before I had told him what it was' uses the past perfect after 'before' to emphasise that the action in the 'before' clause had not yet happened at the main moment.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal of past deduction: 'must have + past participle'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He must have seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before' dùng 'must have + quá khứ phân từ' để diễn đạt một suy luận chắc chắn về một việc đã xảy ra trong quá khứ — một kết luận hợp lý dựa trên bằng chứng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He must have seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before' uses 'must have + past participle' to express a strong deduction about a past event — a logical conclusion drawn from evidence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào lần ghé thăm thứ ba đến cửa hàng tạp hóa nhỏ ở góc phố của tôi tại Edinburgh, chủ cửa hàng đã gọi tôi bằng tên trước khi tôi cho ông ấy biết tên là gì. Ông ấy hẳn đã thấy nó trên một bưu kiện tôi đã lấy hôm trước. Lời chào ngắn — một cái gật đầu, tên tôi, giá của hai món hàng — và đã sẽ trông không có gì đặc biệt với bất kỳ ai đi ngang qua cửa. Tôi đã sống ở ba thành phố trong thập kỷ qua, và tôi có thể đếm trên đầu ngón tay số lần một người bán hàng đã làm điều này ở bất kỳ thành phố nào trong số đó. Bề ngoài, đó không phải là một cử chỉ hào phóng. Nhưng tôi không bao giờ quên nó, và giờ tôi đến cửa hàng đó thay vì siêu thị lớn hơn cách hai con phố ngay cả khi siêu thị nhanh hơn và rẻ hơn một chút.

Nếu bạn chỉ từng sống ở các thành phố nơi bạn vô danh tại mọi quầy thu ngân, trải nghiệm được nhận ra bởi một người bán hàng có thể nghe có vẻ tầm thường, như một mẩu hoài niệm nhỏ về cuộc sống làng quê. Nó không tầm thường. Các thành phố hiện đại được tổ chức quanh việc không được biết đến — bởi nhân viên thu ngân, bởi tài xế xe buýt, bởi dược sĩ mà bạn không đọc thẻ tên. Việc không được biết đến thì tiện lợi, và phần lớn thời gian đó là điều chúng ta đang trả tiền cho. Tuy nhiên, chi phí của sự tiện lợi đó nhỏ và liên tục: một trăm giao dịch nhỏ hàng ngày khẳng định, mà không ai cố ý, rằng không ai xung quanh bạn cần biết bạn là ai. Cửa hàng góc phố làm gián đoạn mô hình đó. Nó không làm gián đoạn một cách kịch tính. Nó chỉ từ chối, ở một nơi nhỏ trên một con phố, tham gia vào quy tắc.

Điều tôi đã dần nhận ra, hai năm vào đó, là việc được biết bởi một người bán hàng không thực sự là về người bán hàng. Đó là về mẩu cơ sở hạ tầng xã hội nhỏ mà một người như ông ấy lặng lẽ duy trì cho người dân ở hai con phố xung quanh. Ông ấy biết ai vừa chuyển đến và ai vừa chuyển đi. Ông ấy biết mẹ ai đang nằm viện và con tuổi teen của ai đang trải qua giai đoạn khó khăn. Không thông tin nào trong số này được đem ra buôn chuyện hay vũ khí hóa; nó chỉ ngồi với ông ấy, như kho cà chua đóng hộp, sẵn sàng khi ai đó cần được hỏi đúng câu. Mặc dù tôi chắc chắn có lý do thực tế để coi trọng siêu thị lớn hơn — giá, chủng loại, giờ mở cửa — điều siêu thị không thể cho tôi là một người duy nhất sẽ nhận ra nếu tôi biến mất ba tuần. Cửa hàng góc phố có thể. Trong một thành phố, đó là điều hiếm hơn nghe có vẻ, và đáng để bảo vệ theo cách nhỏ tôi có thể, đó là tiếp tục đi ngang siêu thị và mua sữa của tôi từ ông ấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'must have + past participle' correctly?
options: He must have seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before.
He must has seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before.
He must of seen it on a parcel I had picked up the day before.
He must have saw it on a parcel I had picked up the day before.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is the modal of past deduction; past participle of 'see' is 'seen'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect correctly after 'before'?
options: The owner called me by my first name before I had told him what it was.
The owner called me by my first name before I have told him what it was.
The owner called me by my first name before I had tell him what it was.
The owner called me by my first name before I had told him what is it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle' ('told'). Indirect questions use statement word order ('what it was').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: Even when the supermarket would be quicker and slightly cheaper, I go to the corner shop.
Even when the supermarket will be quicker and slightly cheaper, I go to the corner shop.
Even when the supermarket would been quicker and slightly cheaper, I go to the corner shop.
Even when the supermarket would being quicker and slightly cheaper, I go to the corner shop.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would + be' is the second conditional pattern for a hypothetical comparison.

options: Although there is nothing dramatic about a small daily greeting, it matters more than it sounds.
Although there is nothing dramatic about a small daily greeting, but it matters more than it sounds.
Although, there is nothing dramatic about a small daily greeting it matters more than it sounds.
There is nothing dramatic about a small daily greeting although however it matters more than it sounds.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'however' as a sentence adverb?
options: However, the cost of that convenience is small and continuous.
However the cost of that convenience is small and continuous.
However; the cost of that convenience is small and continuous.
However, the cost of that convenience is small and continuously.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sentence-initial 'however' takes a comma. 'Continuous' is an adjective parallel to 'small'; the adverb 'continuously' would not fit.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'in preference to + noun' correctly?
options: I now go to that shop in preference to the larger supermarket two streets away.
I now go to that shop in preference of the larger supermarket two streets away.
I now go to that shop in preference for the larger supermarket two streets away.
I now go to that shop in preference at the larger supermarket two streets away.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In preference to + noun' is the standard collocation meaning 'instead of'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'whose' as a relative pronoun?
options: The pharmacist whose name tag you do not read.
The pharmacist who's name tag you do not read.
The pharmacist that name tag you do not read.
The pharmacist which name tag you do not read.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun. 'Who's' is the contraction of 'who is'; 'that' and 'which' cannot show possession.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'a single person who would notice if + past simple'?
options: A single person who would notice if I disappeared for three weeks.
A single person who would notice if I would disappear for three weeks.
A single person who would notice if I disappear for three weeks.
A single person who would noticed if I disappeared for three weeks.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional inside the relative clause: 'would + base verb' in the main and past simple in the if-clause; 'would' does not appear inside the if-clause.

options: Worth defending in the small way I can.
Worth to defend in the small way I can.
Worth defend in the small way I can.
Worthy of defend in the small way I can.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth + -ing form' is the standard structure. 'Worth to' is wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'rarer than it sounds' correctly?
options: In a city, that is rarer than it sounds.
In a city, that is rare than it sounds.
In a city, that is more rare than it sounds.
In a city, that is the rarest than it sounds.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rare' is one-syllable: comparative 'rarer'. 'More rare' is non-standard; superlative 'rarest' is wrong with 'than'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect for the relationship over time correctly?
options: I have never forgotten it.
I have never forget it.
I never have forgotten it.
I has never forgotten it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have + past participle'; the adverb 'never' fits between 'have' and 'forgotten'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If small acts of recognition by a shopkeeper hold a neighbourhood together, what happens when economic pressure replaces those shops with supermarkets and chains? Whose responsibility is it to protect that fragile infrastructure — customers, planners, or the businesses themselves?
